speaker: interviewer
text: Today is the 1st of May, 2014. My name is Stephen Smith. I am with-- with Dario Gabbai. We're in Los Angeles, California, and the language of this interview will be English. This interview is a second interview conducted with Dario Gabbai. The content of this interview assumes all of the content of the first interview given by Mr. Gabbai previously. Dario, thank you very much for agreeing to do a second interview for the USC Shoah Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this interview with you today, what I would like to do is concentrate on the specific aspects of your experience as former sonderkommando at Auschwitz-Birkenau. The first thing I would like to ask you is, could you clarify for me the numbering system of the four crematoria at Auschwitz-Birkenau, and the crematorium in-- crematorium in which you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing that I know is that crematorium 2, where I worked, was the biggest one. 1, 3, and 4 were smaller one, and were about a mile away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, very often you hear about five crematorium-- 1, 2, 3, 4, 5-- in which number 2, 3, 4, and 5 are at Birkenau. But when you refer to crematorium number 2, am I right in thinking that that's what's often referred to as crematorium number 3, because of the-- the one at Auschwitz and the main camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stop one second for the audio [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were just talking about the crematorium numbering system. So if you could just clarify for me which number you were in.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, from the beginning, cremat-- I was in crematorium 2. And that all the time they were talking about crematorium 2. I didn't know anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived at Auschwitz-Birkenau, could you just explain to me the procedure for-- for the selection that you went through on the ramp. Which ramp were you? You arrived straight to Auschwitz-Birkenau, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, we were just on the ramp on the Birkenau. Yeah. And what is the other question?

speaker: interviewer
text: The question was what the procedure was for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The procedure was that they were just taking numbers where you would be, you know, actually, and most of them were signalizing that 1-- 10 times this, 1 times this, you know. 10 times that would be to go direct to-- to-- through the gas chambers, and the others to work. And always came a number of 10% of the overall for working, and the balance, an hour later, they didn't exist anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember that moment of selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I think most of the time I thought was Mengele at-- that was doing the selections. I don't know if it was all the time, but he's one of the principals, you know, doing the selections.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and do you remember who carried out the selection when you were there? Do you remember who was carrying out the selection that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was Mengele carrying the selection at that time, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the date of your arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's 11 days after the [? courthouse. ?] I think-- was a-- was written down. I don't remember, was the 14th, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think on the document it says the 11th of April.

speaker: interviewee
text: 11 of April, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that would be 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just tell me about, once the selection had taken place, do you remember where you were taken to to be processed into the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing that I know is after you are in the ramp, they come in and they process you, you know. I don't remember nothing else. Because after that, when they take you away, you know, you are already in quarantine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do-- do you remember the block number in which you were in quarantine? Do remember where you were in quarantine in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly, but I was in the quarantine for a while, you know. And let me see if I can remember. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When they took us out of the camp to go to the quarantine on this [? place, ?] they would-- would put the number with a pen, with a pen that wrote-- actually was putting down [INAUDIBLE] writing the numbers. And then afterward they finish. At the end, I had a-- I know was swollen for 20 days before it came back to normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you just remind me your number?

speaker: interviewer
text: The tattooing that took place, was that before you were sent to quarantine, or was it once you had arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not before, but after, you know. During the quarantine, you got the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people did you have around you that you knew at that time, that had come on your transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was full of my family, [? Mores ?] Venezia family-- and [? Mores, ?] Venezia, and Shlomo, you know. These are the three that I remember being with us. And we were in the same one, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know them before you arrived at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I knew them, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did having friends and associates like them, did it help you through that process, to have people around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. That there was a long process, was very, very good when you have people who around you, you know, because you don't know where you're going. We [INAUDIBLE] know that we will be killed right away. So that was very good news when you had people around you looking at each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me an example how you might have helped each other or supported each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: By talking. The only thing I can remember is, you know, don't stop talking. Even if you don't know anything, you try to have a conversation of any kind to be able to forget what the-- the balance of your voyage will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would that talking be about figuring out what to do, or would it be about things nothing to do with the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, the situation was very, very intense, when-- to find out-- because everybody was thinking what [INAUDIBLE] are going to be happening, because we didn't know exactly. Once we don't know what our faith will be, always you make assumptions of kinds, that we'll be OK, we will not be OK. We are going to do this, we are going to do that. But nobody knew that we are going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you find that out?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, I have to stop one second again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Begin again. Sorry about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you were in the wagon and on your way, what was conversation about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The conversation-- mostly the conversation we could talk about and would be, where are we going? We're taking time, days and days and days. And we were figuring out-- I was figuring out, what these people want to do with us. So doesn't-- nothing comes out until you are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what conclusions did you draw when you were on the train about the destination?

speaker: interviewee
text: The destination, I don't-- I was [INAUDIBLE] my conclusion that won't be good enough, you know, from seeing what is going on. Because the-- the SS were-- I will say, people that they want to have fights, confusion, you know, with everybody. And-- and we weren't ready for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived at the camp, how did you-- how did you find out what was happening there, and when did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were finding out not too long after what was going on, because people already knew around. And if you see the documentary of ours, you see when-- when Shlomo was asking somebody. And they were showing to him to see up there, you know, what was going on, you know. So we knew that the outcome won't be good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm gonna do it one last time. Sorry. Speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're describing to me how you discovered what was happening at Auschwitz-Birkenau. Was there a moment for you of realization as to how-- where you had arrived, as a-- as a place of execution, of mass killing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't know at that time it was a mass killing. Didn't take too long, because I-- when they selected my-- my brother, the small brother [INAUDIBLE], seeing my mother and father going into the bus, the truck, whatever it was, he ran with them. And then after a day or two I found out that there is no coming back. So we knew very fast what was going on. And there were always some people, that they knew exactly that it was a killing around going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you heard that, how did-- what-- did you accept that, and accept your fate? Or how did you adapt to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, many times we knew that our days were numbered, especially after we went to the sonderkommando, you know. On that-- every the six months of whatever, they used to change them, you know. I was lucky that I was on the last one. But before, every six months they would change the sonderkommando with new ones, because always there were people coming in for the same purpose, you know. But you know, always you go-- I used to go by, if I am alive the next day. That-- that was always my-- my vision, was not to go in front of the Germans, always, because I made up my mind that that war will be ending one day. And I want to be-- if I'm alive, I want to be the guy to tell the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you know you wanted to tell the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, very soon, you know, because I knew that, you know, I didn't have too much time. So I have to find ways to see. You know, I was always observing what was happening. Every day, you know, our-- the Germans were really some-- most of them were very tough people, you know. They didn't want us around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to me the behavior of the Germans before you got to the sonderkommando, just in the main camp itself, and how-- how you observed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have too much to do with them. We were only 30 days of-- and then after the 30 days, we were already in the sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to me how you were transferred to the sonderkommando? Was it a selection that took you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was-- that was a selection. They selected, I think at that time, if I remember, they selected 35 Greeks to go into the sonderkommando. That's all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember that selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, they came one day, and they-- we didn't know. They selected a new one to block 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- where was block 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that block 13 was the sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: And physically, where was that block?

speaker: interviewee
text: Physically?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the block, physically? Was it associated to the gas chamber? Was it in a part of the men's camp? Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was part of the camp. I, you know, I don't know exactly the route or where exactly. But it wasn't too far away, you know. It just.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression of the environment of the gas chamber and the crematoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- they didn't let us know too much about. We knew that we had to work day and night, our 12-hour shift. And you know, when you had that in mind-- and they were always, go, fast, schnell, or whatever it was. We know that-- we knew that was the motto of these people. They wanted to have as much as they could to kill as fast as they-- they can have it. It-- it was become a daily routine for us. And every day was something coming, you know. At that time, it was during the end of the-- of Europe, you know. And when we went in '44, they already killed probably most of them. The biggest one was the Hungarian Jews coming. These were the big one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you started in the sonderkommando? Do you remember the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's I don't remember the day, but it wasn't too far after 30 days of quarantine. They-- they sent us to-- they did whatever they had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how the-- the place looked physically from the outside? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember that we had a-- a garden outside on the sonderkommando, I think. That-- that what I remember. It was nice. They kept it very, very nice all around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a fence around the-- the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think it was a fence. Nobody could come close. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your previous description, you described the undressing room, and the gas chamber being below ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you just describe to me what was above ground, what you-- what you saw physically, the building that was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't really remember. I know it was down. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we couldn't even talk to anyone when they were getting undressed, you know. The SS were very tough. And we-- we couldn't give anybody any suggestions, or tell them what would happen. But-- but I know were up, where they were throwing the gas, just was the German with a couple of people of-- of us, and to open up the 18-inch whatever it was, or the opening to throw the gas was very heavy. And so the Germans used to do it themself, throw in the gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you about your routine, first of all. How did-- how did your day begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the day-- the day begins, you know, just-- you get up. And they give you-- the-- the thing is that the-- the sonderkommando, you know, they had always better delivery of-- of things than the regular ones, because everybody coming, always they brought a few cases or a few boxes of food. And the Germans, they didn't care. You can have anything you want, and anything, undressing like this. So we had that. That was OK. But inside of us wasn't OK, you know. I couldn't stomach, you know. They give us-- it will be better to have the soup and die before time. But seeing that today, for me was better that that I was this way, so I can-- I can--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the living accommodation that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: The living accommodations were better than everybody else. Was on top of the crematorium-- regular bed, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I don't know, what else-- what else can we ask for?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of you were in that living accommodation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember how many of us. But it was a full-- a full people that were doing the business, doing the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were doing shift work. So did you share a bed with another sonderkommando in the shift, or did you have your own bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, maybe. But it was, as far as I know it so many beds, so whatever they had to do. Maybe we-- we slept with-- somebody slept in my bed and I slept in their bed. But-- but it was this, at that time, was the only thing that we were looking-- we were looking where we were better than the other people. And they're going-- but we were better in a certain way, you know. If you sit down and think what we were doing, you know, seeing so many cadavers, you know, go and pick them up, taking them out, put them in the thing, put them in-- to go to the ovens. Everybody-- you know, our minds were very confused.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about how the process worked? So you would-- you would wake in the morning. Would you go straight to work, or would there be some kind of appell during the morning? What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: There is always an appell, you know. And I think everything was outside the appell, you know, if it's cold and everything. But they would continue every day, say, if you are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at what time would you go to work on your shift?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was early, you know, 7. I don't know. I never thought about these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember how you were organized, in terms of who did what function in the sonderkommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. You had your own functions, you know. They-- most of-- of us were taking from the gas chambers, taking them out, and put them in a wagon, put them in the elevator that went to the second floor. And the-- the second floor was somebody else putting them in the oven. [CLEARING THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe you would just talk me through the process of what happened, from when the victims would arrive through the whole process. So what happened first when they arrived? Would you know-- would you know in advance that they were coming, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. You will know that it's coming, 2,500 people, or 1,500, or 2,000. Most of the time were from 15 to 2,500. The big ones were when the-- the Hungarian came in, then because they wanted to get rid of them fast enough. And they only way they could realize is to bring 15,000 a day. It was something that I couldn't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you know that the transport was coming in? Who would announce that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another capo will announce, you know. We didn't have any to do with the Germans. Everything was the inside site of, you know. We had only about two Germans outside. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once this had been announced, did you go to your stations to wait, or how did you anticipate what was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they knew what time were coming and we were ready in the undressing room, and wherever we had to do. And going, actually, we were very, you know, to see that everybody goes into the gas chamber, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you describe to me what happened when the victims came in? Would they have any awareness of where they were and what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you cannot tell anything to anyone. Just undress fast enough. And you were-- you were there to help them undress, to get undressed. They didn't take too long to undress. And then they are getting in line and going to-- when you get into the gas chamber, then a lot of people, they are not all stupid, you know. They-- they were seeing what it was, that no-- no water coming or anything. So it took a while. But after they closed the door, they knew that that was the end of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as the victims came into the undressing room, can you describe to me the atmosphere in that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody-- the atmosphere was very tense. The people, they couldn't-- you couldn't tell them what was going to happen, but they were thinking themselves. And I think they were thinking that-- that their lives were ending, you know. So you could go there in the crematorium, you [INAUDIBLE] is the end of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was informing them about the process of undressing? Who was telling them that they had to undress?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- we were telling them. There were a couple of Germans there, but we were the ones to tell undress, take out everything, you know. And you know, also, people were very distressed to find they were nude, you know, especially the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they display that distress? What were--?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were confused. And I observed, and they were confused. And you couldn't tell them anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did some people refuse to undress?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't-- I did not-- I didn't see anyone. The only thing I saw sometimes were some friends of ours that they came in. And there were also Shlomo, a couple of-- they were relatives. They came in one time, and I-- I told them, look, you better go there. That is taking two minutes and you die. If you go there, it would be five minutes, and you'll suffer, you know. At that time, we told them that there is no way. And one of them asked Shlomo, why don't you tell the German. You know, maybe he can relieve him, leave him alone. And Shlomo, poor guy, said, I don't think I can do anything, but I'll talk to him. And he talked to the guy. And he told him, oh, you shit, something like that, you know, in German. Everything goes through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did have from time to time a German officer in there during the undressing procedure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he giving orders, or was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he-- he-- he was just looking, you know. The orders were given by the capos. If they have any problems, probably he will come in, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and when people were distressed, did they-- did they break down and cry, or did they find comfort with each other? How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was sometimes happening then. You know. Most of them, their inside, you know, everybody is confused. You come to someplace that you don't know what will happen to you. They're trying to find comfort. And how do you find the comfort? There's no such a thing. And you cannot talk to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did-- when you were seeing this procedure day in and day out, how did you feel when you were carrying out those-- those daily routines, and meeting the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always, you know, down. We didn't-- we knew that our days are numbered somehow. But we had to do-- you know, everybody's complaining about our jobs, you know. All the-- if you can talk to survivors, they always will be against us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once people had undressed, who gave the instruction for them to go into the gas chamber, and how did that procedure take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tell you-- they don't tell you much. Go into a line, and they start bringing them in. That didn't take too long, actually, when they-- but first of all, they used to put in, instead of 12 or 1,400, up to 2,000, you know, like sardines. Then they knew that something was wrong when they closed the door, because afterwards, even if you are around there, you see the scratching of the people on the walls. And you know, when they finish you see a lot of blood. That's why you had to paint it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to force people to go in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. If they-- if there's not enough room, you used to push.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anybody ever resist entering into the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't know. But when you are like that, you know, you see that your mother or your son-- or your-- you go in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, there are always people against things that they are not natural.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if somebody did resist, how-- how was-- how were they treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing, [INAUDIBLE] you push them in. This is nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who would push them in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would push them in. The SS, they don't get [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find that at that moment that of-- that people realized that it was-- how lethal the situation was, and that there was an, you know, an outcry of any description?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outcry always, but rebellion, I didn't see it. Where would we go? No, the Germans were-- they let anybody know, if [INAUDIBLE] they will kill you, probably, right away. So nothing happens that I didn't see that. But.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there times when you had more people arrive than you could accommodate in the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: More than the necessary--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had more people than you could actually physically get in the gas chamber. Did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, always. They-- they tried to push as much as they could, the Germans. They want to-- to finish with-- with the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the point at which the gas chamber could not take any more people, were there-- were there times where people had to wait, having already undressed, or did you control it so that they always went?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were pretty good at how much they can put extra, you know. Always they would find a place to push them in. I didn't see anything that-- they didn't have any other way, you know. Where were they going to live? Out? For what? They couldn't put it back in another. Everything was very specific with their numbers, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if I'm-- if I'm hearing you correctly, only the number of people that could fit into the gas chamber were allowed into the undressing room.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. They-- they knew how many. And they knew how many extra could put them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you didn't have the case where you would have people left over.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people-- once they were in the gas chamber, how did they react to being in there? What did you hear? What did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing you hear is-- is the scratching on the walls. That's what I hear myself. They're scratching the walls. They know-- they-- they know all the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were very, you know, also, could you imagine us being there all the time? My-- my mind was blind. I couldn't-- I don't know how I survived. Every [INAUDIBLE] says your mind is still OK. I don't know if it's OK. There is somebody else here-- somebody else inside of me. I'm sorry to focus on the detail, but I would like to just ask you a bit more about the procedure. The door was closed. Who closed the door and sealed-- who sealed the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think-- I don't know. I don't know if it was a German, or the capos did most of the work. You know. They closed the door. I mean, it's not too much. And when you are there, you know, everybody's-- they don't wait too long, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a waiting time between when the door was closed and when the Zyklon B was put into the-- put into chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well they were always ready upstairs to, you know, their signals, you know, you put them in. It doesn't take too long, you know. I mean the-- the Zyklon B, whatever, Zyklon B, when the gas gets with the air or something-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you're finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember how they signaled to the-- the SS that they were ready? Was there a--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I don't. There was a procedure-- very, very, simple, I think. They-- they knew it-- you do it once, twice, a few times, and you know that is a routine going on. Because always it was from 1,500 to 2,500 people. So they-- they knew exactly. They never brought things except when really they-- they think it was necessary. And that is when-- and they accommodate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me the scene in the undressing room once everybody was inside the gas chamber? What did it look like at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: The undressing room? [CHUCKLING] You had to pick up-- we had to pick up everything. Everything goes to Germany or wherever it was, or. The only thing they left was, I think, the-- the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to sort any of, you know, the belongings out at that point, or did you just bundle it all together? How did it work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think they used to put it in trucks or whatever it was. They didn't-- they were pretty organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you be involved in clearing the clothes from the undressing room in one of your roles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't do it, but they did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They being other sonderkommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever any people that tried to hide, for example, or hide children, or any of that sort of-- amongst the clothes, I mean. Did that ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But we hear a couple of times, you know, that there were somebody in there they didn't know. It was a-- it was a very young child or something. I think once or twice I heard some, because sometime, you know, after the-- there were-- the-- the gas came in. And a couple of times we went in, we see maybe a very-- not me, but [? Morris ?] once saw a very small guy that was alive after, you know, because probably the-- the mother wanted to catch up and-- and maybe he can save it. But nothing happened. The German came in, gave him a shot, and boom, I'm finished, you know. They were very-- the Germans were very good at executing people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just to clarify, you're saying that there was one or two times when there was a small child in the undressing area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was in the-- the gas chambers. The undressing area, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on a-- on a very rare occasion, a child might survive the gassing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the Germans would come in and kill him-- finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did the gassing procedure last for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 30 minutes, because they-- they were waiting for us to come in. The-- the ventilation should start. And when it was OK for us to come in, they will open the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you observe ever the SS placing the Zyklon B into the chamber from the roof level?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I think Shlomo once was up there helping the SS. I tried to stay out as much as I could from the-- from the eyes of the Germans. I don't think I could survive. Because my main game at that point, that I knew that my days were numbered, is to be able to get the-- not get the attention of the Germans, but for me to be in a-- in a way out of them, to be for the next day and the next day and the next day. Like I told you before, you know, that war is going to end one day. It couldn't be forever. And when that ends, I'd like to be alive and be able to-- to tell the story, which I was happy to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the process of the gassing, Dario, can you just describe to me the-- how the sounds changed? Did you know when the gassing had been successful from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh it's always successful because they just waited 30 minutes. They knew that it takes from 2 to 5 minutes, depending where they are. There's no-- and they're through, you know. So they waited-- they way 30 minutes to be able to open the doors and put the ventilation on so we can go and do our job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people-- once they realized what was happening to them, did they scream?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were screaming yeah. Yeah, they were. A lot of crying, a lot of, you know-- could you imagine, you are with all your family there, and you know you are dying? You know, it just doesn't take too long. That's why if you knew somebody, we tell them, you go there, where it's two minutes. In two minutes you are gone. If not, you go to the five minutes, you are going to three minutes of suffering is a big deal. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Could you imagine that? Now that I'm thinking to tell people you will die in three minutes or two minutes or five minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you know that-- that the process had been successful at that point? Would it go quiet? How did you know? Or was it just a matter of timing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. When you are dead, well you're going to-- you are going to cry no more. You are going to [INAUDIBLE] can tell anything. And the process was, as far as I knew, that it would take from 2 to 5 minutes. After, very seldom you would find somebody that was alive. [? And ?] you never find somebody is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who decided when to switch the ventilation system on? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. All these things, the capos are doing most of the things. We were not getting involved too much in--

speaker: interviewer
text: And while the gassing was taking place, what were the sonderkommando doing? Did they have duties that they were carrying out at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were busy doing something-- cleaning, you know, where there's a lot of people over there that left everything there. So some of the food always we took out for ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've heard in previous descriptions that from time to time, the SS would observe through some kind of peephole--

speaker: interviewee
text: How they-- how they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a peephole in the gas chamber door?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever watch?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [INAUDIBLE]. Tough, tough things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who opened the gas chamber door? Who was responsible for opening the door after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always the capos were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would the sight, the scene be when you opened the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a-- a very terrific scene. You see all from regular people, you see just coming in a few minutes ago. You open the door after 30 minutes, and you see a [INAUDIBLE] 1,500 2,000, 2,500, all dead. This is always that my mind, because it is a very, very hard view to see people that you saw them before, and all of a sudden you see them 5, 10 minutes late, they are all dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you know, with the gas-- with the gas they get blue, and they get, you know, you have to work hard to untie them to be able to drag them out, put them in the thing, to put them in the elevator.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you describe to me-- were they are on the floor, were they-- how did they-- how did-- how did that scene look?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it looks, you know, they are like this, like that. Always they are bending. They stay up, you know, when the gas [INAUDIBLE] goes down. I don't know, [INAUDIBLE] different things, the place of the body. But we have to untangle everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you go about that work of untangling? How did you-- how did you physically do that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to be powerful to get even-- if they were like this, together, you know, or [INAUDIBLE] were a few people. We are not alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of the sonderkommando would go in to carry that out? How many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many. A few-- a few. Four, five of them, I think. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Always were-- were people helping us. They want to get rid fast, put them to the ovens so that we can paint it and finish with it. So maybe another transport was coming, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any tools or any implements to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a-- we did a-- a cane, and a-- [INAUDIBLE] think, a strap or whatever. But always [INAUDIBLE] things there to-- to work, and-- that [INAUDIBLE] take them to be ready to be-- to be go to the ovens.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the purpose of the cane? How did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Put the cane up in here, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you would pull the cadaver out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did it-- how far did you have to take the cadaver from the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- well, it put it-- put it in a stretcher, whatever it was, you know, to take it to the elevator. But we had a few people taking one or two at a time to the elevator.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the stretchers, what were they made of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you-- you would be working inside the gas chamber, and placing the corpses on the stretcher. And then you would leave the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think we were taking them out first. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some, we didn't have to put in a stretcher. We just move around to the elevator. It was not too far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how far the elevator was from the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. It was just a-- a few minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you would place the corpse where? [PHONE RINGING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that mine?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe for me how the bodies in the crematorium were cremated? What was the procedure, precisely?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only procedure is we take the bodies up to the elevator, to the second floor, and to the ovens. And people are there doing their job, put-- putting-- we have nothing, anything to do when we are there. So they would put it in a stretcher, put it inside the oven, and get cremated. Every 20 minutes you-- they turn around, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many corpses would you put on a single-- in a single time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I think about four or six. I don't know, four or six. Four. And after 20 minutes you turn them around, and 30 minutes. And then you put more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many of you were working in that particular area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were just putting the corpse up to the elevator to the ovens. And then the-- the other people there-- two, three of them-- they take over. And we leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- the capos that were working with you-- did you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew their names. I don't remember them now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- what was the relationship like between the capos and the regular sonderkommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were pretty good, the relationships, you know, about with us, because I think they were the ones-- that there were a couple of them, they were Germans or something, and-- [INAUDIBLE]. But most of the capos, they were with us in the number 2, crematorium 2, were pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how the capos were selected to become capos?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's not. We already-- when I was there, there was already capos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you ever considered to be a capo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many of them there were, approximately, capos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, there were, you know, in each crematorium, there were-- there were about two or three. And there were general capo was one by the name of Kaminsky that was a-- was very good one. And at they end, they kill him. The-- the SS kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say he was a good capo, what-- what do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he never-- whenever the Germans asked him, he never revealed names or anything else, you know. So he was a pretty good man. But it didn't last at-- at the end. After the revolution, I think, they didn't-- he-- the Germans were talking to him, but he never revealed any names, whoever was-- who was in the revolution, who did that with [INAUDIBLE]. And finally they put him in the oven himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I ask you about the role of the SS? Did you know any of the names of the SS that were associated to your crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we had only two SS. One of them was from Holland, which is pretty-- was good enough. And the other was a very bad man, you know, that I know [INAUDIBLE]. We didn't have any Germans to-- to do. They were outside, sitting outside-- nothing to do. But one was a terrible guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The other was from Holland. I don't even remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me-- when you say they were terrible, can you describe to me their-- their behaviors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very bad behavior, you know. He was not a nice man. Anything that they find you, you did something, he would kill you, or-- or give you-- I don't know, just very bad man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would he do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How would he carry out those killings, the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I haven't seen him killing. But it was his behavior-- everyone around knew that he wasn't a good man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you deal with that as a-- as a sonderkommando? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because, we didn't ever have nothing to do with him, you know. He was outside sitting. We had two people at the time, you know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everything was done by the capos-- everything. They were the people that we tried to be in a good name.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a previous conversation, you described to me the-- something that you either knew about or had seen, I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Something that you either knew about, or you had seen, where you described to me about some shooting practice with one of the SS. Do you remember describing that to me? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With-- with younger women.

speaker: interviewee
text: They only one I know about that was Moll, I think-- Otto Moll. Otto Moll was a very, very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sadistic man. And he will-- when they were coming in, you know, they would pick up about 20 young girls, you know, and just undress them, and try to shoot them in the-- in the-- in the places, you know, what they call them-- just the breasts, and here. So that was Moll.

speaker: interviewer
text: So always from-- he was the guy bringing the people to-- to the ramp. And from there, always he would come, 1, 1:30 in the morning, you know, and taking these girls upstairs and-- sadistic guy. He'd just do whatever he could. And they would kill him-- he would kill them-- nice, beautiful young men-- girls. How did you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was there. I was seeing when he was coming and everything else. I, you know, he was coming always 1, 1:30 in the morning. And I would see the motorcycle coming in, and I knew that is Moll, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you view that from? Where were you at the time when you saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there, you know. I was-- I was working at the time. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He-- he-- I think I wrote a little bit about him, you know. He was very, very sadistic man. And I don't-- I don't know how he used to do that. And the-- the other problem with this kind of people is after work, they used to go to their families not too far away, a couple of miles from-- from the camp, and having-- having a wife and children. And I could-- I couldn't think or believe that a guy of this nature would do such things to-- to the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any other acts of sexual violence against women?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or men?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was one individual that you did witness.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Moll's role? Do you know what his overall role was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well he used to bring the people from-- from the station, you know, to the crematorium

speaker: interviewer
text: And where would he carry out these shootings?

speaker: interviewee
text: In front of the people that are there. Just-- very, very, very bad. I don't even like to tell the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it's important to tell the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I always told the story about Moll-- Otto Moll. Always. I wrote about and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back on the experience of working in the sonderkommando, do you have-- what are your feelings about that period of time? How do you process that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was a very, very bad feelings. All of the people that work in the sonderkommando, the feelings that you get in-- in your soul. And then you don't know what to do. Always-- I always, at night, many times, I will-- and the only thing I will think is Moll. Because he is the-- the people that I observe. And I don't know, and I don't-- don't think that were pretty nice things to do. I don't care who you are in society.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this is what-- you know, not all the Germans are-- you know, I have nothing. I have bad feelings, but I don't-- I don't, you know, what is the word? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Anyhow, I-- I don't-- I don't I approve these type of things going around the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were looking at there. And they did so many things that were not-- you know, they were killing the people. What do you have to play with them, and having taken young girls and shooting them [INAUDIBLE] here and here and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel any sense of responsibility for what occurred at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- responsibility, no. I-- I know people, there-- there are a lot of people who are survivors, they don't care about us at all, you know. But who-- we couldn't do anything else? I think you have a few pieces that you saw of rabbis and things, probably, and [INAUDIBLE]. They say about, you know, what-- but I can do nothing anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I ask you for a moment or two about the revolt that occurred, and how-- and the steps that were taken? Were you aware of any of the planning at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had there about 10 Russian people. They were in the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were in the Russian army. And these are the ones. And with one Greek guy, [INAUDIBLE] Baruch, that they put together, you know, a scheme how to be able to make a revolt in the sonderkommando. This is what I know, that they, they were in Block 2 first, I think. And they put the scheme. And that Greek guy was a captain in the Greek army. And they-- but we didn't-- they-- they put a date for the revolution, you know. I think it was 19 of October. I have all the-- and it didn't happen because the first one was because of the-- were the people on the-- on the thing, they were on the-- I don't know, I can't remember the name of the place. They were-- they were outside-- the Germans were [INAUDIBLE] keeping the-- an eye on the-- on the-- on the camp, and they-- they couldn't make it. Something happens, and we abandoned that date of revolution. So they put a newer [INAUDIBLE]. But the only thing I-- I know, I think I-- you can read it. You'll find out exactly what happened. Because what they-- we saw is that 1:30 in the morning one day, we see flames coming from crematorium 3. And we didn't know what it was. But we were in crematorium 2 always ready to-- to be part of it. And so-- but the-- the-- the Polish guy that were in our number 2 didn't let us do anything. So we didn't do anything. But they did theirs on 3 and 4. And I think they burned number 4 crematorium. And there were about the only thing we knew that in the morning, you know, they tried to-- to get out, and-- and maybe get rid of the camp and go. And they couldn't do it, because the SS were there. And they killed 600 of them. And in the morning we had to put them in the oven ourselves. That's the only thing I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- after they killed the sonderkommando from the other crematoria, then your unit then had to cremate the-- the executed sonderkommando from the other crematoria. You were given the job of cremating the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --other sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the people-- to the people that did the revolution and the other ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you saw or when you heard about what had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I felt that we wanted to be part of it. And to-- and we couldn't do it, because the-- the Germans-- when-- you know, the Polish guys in number 2, they didn't want to have no part of it, because we weren't there in the beginning, so what for? And that what happens. And so. But they-- they pay with their lives, 600 of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: We know now from looking back on history that a number of sonderkommando kept notes about their experience and-- and hid them. Did you ever know anybody that was involved in trying to document what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is-- is a few of them that-- they don't exist now. One was a [INAUDIBLE]. The other was Cohen, you know, the guy. These two, they wrote plenty of it, you know. There is a-- there is a big one. Somebody sent me-- before he died, Mario [INAUDIBLE] sent me a note of the son of-- what's his first name-- from Cohen? Wrote-- I have it in Greek, but I wrote about the sonderkommando, about the revolution. I don't know. I-- you can-- I can give it to you. You can [INAUDIBLE] and do it-- you can do the translation from Greek to English.

speaker: interviewer
text: How aware were you personally of the-- of the significance of the history that you were involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the-- about the revolution?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, sorry. About the Final Solution, and the killing that was taking place at Auschwitz. Were you aware of how historically significant it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? When? During that time? You mean, when I was there? Always was very important to me, you know, when they were killing such-- in such a way, you know. This is now part of our-- of our history, of our taking care of ourselves. No, it was just, how can you do such a thing? I-- I can never approve such a thing in-- in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware of being an eyewitness to the Final Solution at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was aware, yeah. I was, sure, aware of the Final Solution. And the thing is, you know, Hitler didn't want to stop. [INAUDIBLE] want to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what makes me fear is that from 1945 to-- [INAUDIBLE] was the end-- 1933 to 1945, 12 years, Hitler, with the Final Solution killed millions of people. How can it be? How come with that one guy, you know, put that through in such a way, with all this? It's-- it's impossible of the mind of the people or a human being to put together, you know. They put together so many things. How they did it? From the-- you know, they pick up the Jews, pick up, the-- what are the other ones? The-- Gypsies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gypsies, yeah. How can-- full of millions of people, you know, to be dead, you know, for what? What did they do? They killed themselves at the end. So I-- I can't approve such a thing for any reason, there is no such a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware during the time, and did you see coming into the-- the facility, the crematorium 2, people other than Jews? Were all of the victims Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, most-- yeah. Where I was at, the only thing I knew were only Jewish people coming in. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware that there were-- were other victims, too, at that point, or was that something that you were aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the crematorium, I don't think there were no other people, you know. If there were, might be few that I don't know. But 99.9% were Jewish people. From-- that is that Hitler wanted to eliminate from life were that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I can ask you just a few questions about your life after the Holocaust and the Second World War. First of all, can I ask you about how you coped with being a Holocaust survivor? What-- how did you get back into life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I got into life very fast, you know. I-- when I went from-- when I got from the camps, and I got liberated, I was liberated with Shlomo, you know. But Shlomo got sick on our way going to-- to Rome. And they took him up to the-- the facility to-- where he couldn't breath or something. He stayed there for a few years, you know, got well, and then got back to Rome, when he met [? Marica ?] and got married and they had five beautiful children. I-- so I from there, I went to Rome myself, and I-- I from Rome I went to Greece, to Athens, where I worked for four years for the American Joint Distribution Committee at that time. You know, at that time Athens was the biggest warehouse after the war where they used to come from all over Europe and go to places. And so they were distributing all this the American Joint Distribution Committee was sending there. Were distributing the-- all [INAUDIBLE] to get dress and everything else. And from-- from there I-- after four years, they-- they finished the work. And I was an Italian citizen, you know. And I didn't think in Greece I couldn't get work at that time. I went to the American embassy, and in the same day they gave me the stamp to-- to go-- to come to the US. In the beginning, I-- they-- the Jewish community of Cleveland OK that. So I went to Cleveland, Ohio. It wasn't my place. [CHUCKLING] It was-- it was cold and [INAUDIBLE]. So I stayed a month. Then I had some friends calling back to-- to Los Angeles. Do you know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nico [? Minardos? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- anyhow, he was a nice man. Passed away a few years ago. And we were-- [INAUDIBLE] helped me a lot to come to Los Angeles, because she knew a lot of influential Jewish people. And I came in here, and I-- I went to work for a textile company called Lensol Fabrics. So Lensol Fabrics, I went to. They had drapery and upholstery fabrics. And at that time, in Los Angeles there were thousands of apartments putting together for-- for-- to rented. And we used-- we used to go-- Lensol used to put a close-out business. And I was director of it. And I used to go all around the-- the US and buy millions of yards of different [? people. ?] And I used to-- I made him rich, the people. And Lensol, with Lensol promotional sales. And I worked there for 35 years. 35 years, I made them rich. And I had-- we had a deal with the two brothers-- Lenny and Saul-- that whatever-- I bought a 65,000-square-foot warehouse in 1620 San Pedro Street in Los Angeles. And I used to go all over the US and buy all [? people-- ?] people they didn't want, and buy-- and they were $3, $4 a yard regular, and I bought it for $0.35.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was working with them, and I used to sell thousands and thousands of yards of fabrics in the LA area. And-- and so Lensol was doing very well. I-- when I went to Lensol, it was doing under 1,000 a year, and when I left they were doing, you know, about 10 million a [? day. ?] But I had a deal with them that whatever they will buy, and they're going to sell from the buying and the selling, the difference, I will get 7 and 1/2%. So one day-- you know-- and I was very tough at that time. One of the brother, the bigger brother came in. And I said, Lenny, what about the building? They sold the building to the two kids for 2 million. So my [INAUDIBLE] 7 and 1/2% is supposed to be 287,000. When I told him, what about the building? Which building, he tell me, [? he ?] [? said. ?] So go to my brother. I said the heck with you, and I-- I left them after 35 years. I left. For you as a Holocaust survivor, when you-- immediately after the Holocaust was over, how would you describe your state of mind at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- for a few years, I didn't talk to anyone, really. But I-- I tried to get into the swing of life. I-- I was married for 24 years, and I-- then I got involved with a few young girls, you know. I always joked, every one lady friend that I had, when coming to me, you know, we got together, always I was 25 years younger. She was 25 years younger than I was. I want to go to the restroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to cut, for a second. So you were just telling me as we had a little break then-- you just telling me about the fact that you had young women, 25 years your junior.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- you know, everybody thinks that I'm younger, that I look. Even today, when I go, they say, you are not 92. You are 69 to 70. [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you live your life? I mean, what were the things that excited you about life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, in 1938, I went-- I was in an Italian school in Thessaloniki, in Salonika. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that time, I wanted to go to Padua to become a doctor. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After that, Mussolini and Hitler got together through the Axis, and they didn't let any more Italian Jews go to universities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So 1939 came in. Everything was [INAUDIBLE] everything else. And there is nothing else I can do, you know. So I always started. When I left the camps, and I went to work, always had in mind to go for many years, just roaming around Europe, or wherever it was, you know. And that's what I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: With TWA, I had half a million miles. Everything first class for 35 years. And that I learned tremendous things going to different places, you know. And always you'll find-- you know, I was married for 24 years. And then I-- we got divorced. And we talk today every day, you know. But at that time, you know, I wanted from the camps, I wanted the freedom to do everything I want, you know, without too many restrictions. And I didn't want children either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you not want children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think whatever I saw, you know, I was very shocked about. And that's what I-- we got a daughter that [INAUDIBLE]. So that was part of my life. But I enjoyed-- my best way in life were from the late-- middle 40s to middle 80s. That's when I really-- I grasped as much as I could from life. That's what I can tell you. I-- I made money. Not too much, but I made a good living. And whatever-- you know, you don't know me. Whatever I had, always I gave it away. So I have nothing now. I live on Social Security.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how did the Holocaust affect those behaviors, do you think? What was that-- what was it about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what was it about? About restrictions, death, you know. And I want the freedom of me, to do anything I want, you know. And I accomplished that, I think. Now, you know, I didn't realize I would be like this, not to work enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think that your drive to roam the world was linked to the captivity that you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. Part of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Part of it, you know. That was a terrible time in life. And I-- I was lucky to go in the camps to the end of it. Other people stayed many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think your relationships with women, and your sexual relationships were also a part of that same--

speaker: interviewee
text: Always I had sexual-- I was very good in that. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't linked to your Holocaust experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which experience?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your Holocaust experience-- it wasn't a sort of a drive, an urge that came from it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Women-- this you have it, I think. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you-- let's talk for a moment or two about memories, as well, then. So when you reflect back on that period, are there any particular memories? Or, for example, a smell that you smell that relates to it, or there's something you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The smell-- the smell of the bodies, or something like that? Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not too much. But, there is some connections with that. I can-- I can smell very good when there's something. But I don't think there is too much to do with it, what happened in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you experienced nightmares as a result of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nightmares? A lot of them. And especially a few years in the beginning, you know. I used to come, you know. If you read Maria Modiano's letter, you'll see that I-- I used to come out at night and scream and things like that from the nightmares.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what they were, the nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nightmares always from the camps. You know, nobody leaves you. Whatever is inside of me from that period never leaves you. Always, always I have you in, attached. That's why I went wild in the world, you know, whatever I could do to always-- I'd never stay home. Always I was traveling. I was always busy. My mind was busy, not because-- whenever I used to go, you know, for many, many years I used to go to the colleges all around here, to the universities. Whenever I finished that, for one week I was-- always something at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would that affect you? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It affects me-- I don't know. I don't know how. I'm always shaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even now. Why-- why the-- the Germans that I-- they don't give me enough to be able to go-- you know, $500 a month. What? I mean, it's nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The nightmares that you experienced, was it about-- was about what you did? Was it about what you saw? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's whatever I saw. Whatever I did doesn't-- I don't want to reapply that to me, because I had nothing to do with it, you know. The thing is, if you didn't do that, you wouldn't be alive. And I tried always to be alive by the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the things that you saw, how would they impress on your mind? Would they come to you during the day as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, whenever I go and tell the stories, that comes to me for at least a week, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what ways, Dario? How-- how would you-- how would you feel that or experience it? Do you relive the experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you relive the experience in your mind? What happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I relive it whenever I see it. When I go to the all the, you know, to the universities, and I tell, when I tell the story, [CLEARING THROAT] it gives you-- I [? think ?] of that time. And it infringes in your mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did you have people that you could  talk to about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I talk to people when I go. And always I talk about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anybody that you could talk to about the memories and the nightmares? Or was that something you had to cope with yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that I cope with myself, you know. I can't give-- I can't give bad things to others.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever seek any professional help for things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want professional help on these kind of things. They do me worse. They do me-- they cannot help me on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why do you think that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I know myself. And I know-- I know the people. They give you. They are going to start putting me more things in my mind that I don't have.

text: When did you begin to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I begin to tell my story-- it-- it [? close-- ?] when after-- at least-- at least 15 years after I came back. Nobody wants to listen in the beginning to anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There's more now than it was in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me some of the ways in which you have gone about telling your story? How have you done that?

speaker: interviewee

And I used to-- to go-- going walking at night in 25 below 0. [INAUDIBLE]. Always I said, oh, beautiful Athens with the sunshine, you know.

text: I think you've also been involved in education activities as well. Can you talk a little bit about some of your experiences with young people, and why-- why you choose to work with young people?

So he took them-- he took them all to-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Florida, and the other people, they took it in the Midwest. And today they're teaching that in the Midwest and Florida. And I don't recognize anything. And I don't have anybody there that I know. The-- the Jewish community of Salonika before the war was 70,000 people. Today there is maybe 1,000 or 1,500.
